articles.title: Costco Just Launched This Fancy Milk That Fans Have Been Clamoring For—Here’s What the Buzz Is All About
articles.content:
好市多（Costco）购物者们不遗余力地为他们希望在这个仓储巨头货架上看到的产品进行宣传活动。有人想要恢复喜爱的烘焙食品，有人正在等待季节性零食回归，而在乳制品区的某个角落，有人坚信如果好市多能销售某种特定的牛奶，他们的生活将会显著改善。显然，坚持终有回报。

好市多以悄然倾听顾客心声、悄然下架产品而闻名，往往在这些产品下架后突然引发热议。最近，这家仓储巨头再次做到了这一点，推出了一款已经让粉丝们兴奋不已的新品，引发了比较帖子的热潮，并让购物者们踏上了“我的门店有货了吗？”的探寻之旅。

这款新品并不花哨，但对于某些购物者来说，这已经是期待已久的时刻。如果网上的反应能说明什么的话，好市多可能刚刚又挖掘出了一个成功的想法。要全面了解这款新的乳制品——以及为什么它让购物者们如此兴奋——我采访了食品科学顾问埃德·麦考密克（Ed McCormick），他是即将出版的《美国人正在问的食品问题》一书的作者。让我详细道来。

这款新品是Kirkland Signature超滤减脂牛奶，一种无乳糖的2%牛奶，许多购物者立即开始将其与热门品牌Fairlife进行比较。该产品每份含有13克蛋白质，营养数据让购物者们表示与Fairlife受欢迎的2%版本惊人地接近。虽然好市多已经销售其他无乳糖牛奶，如Kirkland有机无乳糖牛奶，但这款新产品被定位为高蛋白替代品，具有不同的营养成分。

超滤牛奶最初是普通牛奶，但经过专门的过滤工艺改变了其成分。“超滤意味着牛奶通过极薄的过滤膜进行处理，根据分子大小分离牛奶成分，”麦考密克解释道。“该工艺使牛奶制造商能够浓缩蛋白质和钙等我们知道对健康有益的成分……同时减少乳糖和一些天然糖分。”

这种过滤工艺与奶酪和其他乳制品行业使用的步骤类似。“水、乳糖和一些矿物质等分子更容易通过，而较大的蛋白质则被保留并浓缩，”麦考密克说道。最终产品仍然是牛奶，但营养成分已经改变。

超滤牛奶在乳制品货架上看起来可能和其他牛奶没什么区别，但其日益流行取决于包装内的内容。超滤牛奶已从特色产品稳步发展成为主流类别，受高蛋白饮食、宏量营养素追踪和无乳糖产品需求推动。以下是原因。

最大的卖点之一是营养成分。与普通2%牛奶相比，超滤品种通常提供更高的蛋白质和更低的糖分。作为比较，普通2%牛奶每杯通常含有约8克蛋白质和约12克糖，而好市多的超滤牛奶将蛋白质提升至13克，糖分降至6克——卡路里没有显著增加。

“蛋白质浓缩是最重要的卖点之一，因为许多消费者现在将蛋白质与饱腹感、健身和健康衰老联系在一起，”麦考密克说道。蛋白质支持饱腹感和肌肉维持，尤其是在老年人和运动员中，他说这种低糖特性对消费者非常有吸引力。

超滤牛奶的无乳糖特性对某些消费者来说不仅仅是一个额外好处；对于乳糖不耐受的人来说，这可能是决定性因素。麦考密克说，对乳糖不耐受的人来说，这可能意味着减少腹胀、胀气和消化不适等问题。据麦考密克称，大部分乳糖在过滤过程中自然去除，制造商通常添加分解任何残留乳糖的酶。

许多购物者形容超滤牛奶更浓郁、更奶油或更甜。“甜味部分来自乳糖分解，”麦考密克解释道。“当乳糖分解为葡萄糖和半乳糖时，这些糖对人类味觉来说更甜。”至于质地，正是更高的蛋白质浓度导致了更奶油的口感，他说。

超滤牛奶的另一个主要优点——尤其是对批量购买乳制品的好市多购物者来说——是更长的保质期。根据麦考密克的说法，大多数超滤牛奶使用超高温（UHT）系统和高度受控的条件进行加工，这大大减少了变质。

那么超滤牛奶的保质期是多久？未开封通常可持续约45天，远长于传统巴氏杀菌牛奶的大约两周。这对于不快速消耗牛奶的家庭特别有吸引力。

如果你经常直接喝牛奶或配麦片食用，营养升级可以是一个轻松的选择。不过麦考密克指出，“对于大多数日常烹饪和饮用，消费者可能不会注意到除了更奶油质地和略甜味道之外的明显差异。”

而且由于其更高的蛋白质含量和奶油质地，他说超滤牛奶在咖啡、奶昔、烘焙食品和汤品等应用中表现尤为出色。超滤牛奶仍然可以替代大多数食谱中的普通牛奶，但根据麦考密克的说法，在某些情况下传统牛奶可能更可取。

你可能需要耐心等待。虽然该产品于4月首次出现在德克萨斯州的好市多货架上，并在南加州被发现，但尚未在全国推广。

在Reddit上，购物者们真的很兴奋。有人说：“终于等到了！好市多终于卖超滤牛奶了！”还有人说：“价格太划算了，Fairlife贵死了。”

当然，并非每条评论都是正面的。少数购物者表示他们注意到了不同的余味。“这种牛奶味道太可怕了！淡如水！无法与Fairlife或Mootopia相比，”一位不满意顾客在Reddit上说道。

这可能是好市多真正获胜的地方。

Kirkland版本据报道售价约为10.59至11美元三桶64盎司（总计1.5加仑）。可比的Fairlife牛奶在传统超市通常售价约为5至8美元52盎司（不到半加仑）。换句话说，好市多购物者以仓储批发价格获得了高端牛奶——这是该产品引起如此多关注的主要原因之一。购物者们迅速指出了其价值。

当一款产品引发如此多的讨论——尤其是在好市多圈子里——这通常意味着购物者认为他们找到了一款值得囤货的产品。对于追求更多蛋白质、更低糖分、无乳糖选项或只是想要更划算交易的人们来说，好市多可能刚刚提供了他们一直在等待的东西。
articles.summary:
Costco shoppers tirelessly campaign for products they want to see on the warehouse giant’s shelves. One person wants a favorite bakery item back, another is waiting for a seasonal snack to return, and somewhere in the dairy aisle, someone just knows their life will dramatically improve if Costco carried a specific kind of milk. Apparently, persistence pays off.

Costco has built a reputation for quietly listening to shoppers and dropping products with little fanfare, only for everyone to suddenly be talking about them. Recently, the warehouse giant did it once again with an addition that’s already sparking excitement among fans, inspiring comparison posts and sending shoppers on “Did my store get it yet?” missions.

The newest arrival isn’t flashy, but for certain shoppers, this has been a long time coming. And if the online reaction is any indication, Costco may have just milked another winning idea. To get the full scoop on this new dairy drop—and why it’s got shoppers so fired up—I spoke with food science consultant Ed McCormick, author of the upcoming book The Food Questions America Is Asking. Let’s pour out the details.

Get Reader’s Digest’s Read Up newsletter for more Costco news, cleaning, humor, travel, tech and fun facts all week long.

What kind of milk did Costco just introduce?

The new addition is Kirkland Signature Ultra-Filtered Reduced Fat Milk, a lactose-free 2% milk that many shoppers immediately started comparing to cult favorite Fairlife. The product contains 13 grams of protein per serving and has nutrition numbers that shoppers say are remarkably close to Fairlife’s popular 2% version. While Costco already carries other lactose-free milks, such as Kirkland Organic Lactose Free, this new option is positioned as a higher-protein alternative with a different nutritional profile.

What is ultra-filtered milk?

Ultra-filtered milk starts out as regular milk, but it undergoes a specialized filtration process that changes its composition. “Ultra-filtration means the milk is passed through extremely thin filtration membranes that separate milk components by size,” McCormick says. “The procedure enables makers of milk to concentrate ingredients like protein and calcium that we know are great for good health … while reducing lactose and some of the natural sugars present in milk.”

The filtration is similar to the steps used in the cheese and other dairy industries. “Molecules such as water, lactose and some minerals can pass more easily, while larger proteins are retained and concentrated,” McCormick says. The end result is still milk, but with a modified nutritional profile.

What’s so special about ultra-filtered milk?

Ultra-filtered milk may look like any other carton in the dairy aisle, but its growing popularity comes down to what’s inside the packaging. Ultra-filtered milk has steadily moved from niche product to mainstream category, driven by high-protein diets, macro tracking and demand for lactose-free options. Here’s why.

It has more protein and less sugar

One of the biggest draws is the nutritional profile. Compared with standard 2% milk, ultra-filtered varieties generally offer:

  • About 50% more protein
  • About 50% less sugar
  • Similar calcium levels
  • Similar fat levels, depending on the variety

For comparison, regular 2% milk typically contains around 8 grams of protein and roughly 12 grams of sugar per cup, whereas Costco’s ultra-filtered milk boosts protein to 13 grams and has 6 grams of sugar—without a dramatic increase in calories.

“The concentration of protein is one of the most significant selling points because many consumers now associate protein with fullness, fitness and healthy aging,” McCormick says. Protein supports fullness and muscle maintenance, especially in older adults and athletes, and that and the lower sugar profile can be very appealing to consumers, he adds.

It’s lactose-free

Ultra-filtered milk’s lactose-free status isn’t simply a bonus for some consumers; it can be the deciding factor. For people who are lactose intolerant, McCormick says, this can mean fewer issues such as bloating, gas and digestive discomfort. According to McCormick, much of the lactose is naturally removed during filtration, and manufacturers commonly add enzymes that break down any remaining lactose.

It tastes creamier and sweeter

Many shoppers describe ultra-filtered milk as richer, creamier or sweeter. “The sweetness comes in part from lactose breakdown,” McCormick explains. “When lactose is broken down into glucose and galactose, those sugars are sweeter to the human palate.” As for the consistency, it’s the higher protein concentration that contributes to a creamier mouthfeel, he says.

It lasts longer in the fridge

Another major perk of ultra-filtered milk—especially for Costco shoppers buying dairy in bulk—is extended shelf life. Most ultra-filtered milk is typically processed using ultra-high-temperature (UHT) systems and under highly controlled conditions, which dramatically reduce spoilage, according to McCormick.

So what is ultra-filtered milk’s shelf life? It typically lasts about 45 days unopened, which is much longer than the roughly two weeks or so you get from conventionally pasteurized milk. That makes it especially appealing for households that don’t go through milk quickly.

What should you use ultra-filtered milk for?

If you regularly drink milk straight from the glass or with cereal, the nutrition upgrade can be an easy win. Though McCormick notes, “Consumers would likely not notice large differences beyond a creamier texture and slightly sweeter taste for most everyday cooking and drinking.”

And because of its higher protein content and creamy texture, he says, ultra-filtered milk performs especially well in several applications:

  • Protein shakes and smoothies: The added protein gives drinks more staying power without needing extra ingredients.
  • Coffee drinks and lattes: The creamier mouthfeel can create a richer texture.
  • Overnight oats: Higher protein means a more filling breakfast.
  • Creamy soups and sauces: Extra protein and concentration can add body and richness.

What shouldn’t you use ultra-filtered milk for?

Ultra-filtered milk still works as a substitute in most recipes, but there are a few situations where traditional milk may be preferable, according to McCormick.

  • Sensitive baking recipes: “The modified protein and sugar profiles could slightly affect browning [and] texture,” he notes.
  • Delicate custards: Higher protein content can subtly affect texture.
  • Recipes where exact browning matters: “The limited supply of lactose could affect your Maillard browning reaction,” he says, meaning certain dishes may brown differently.

When will Costco’s ultra-filtered milk be on shelves?

You might have to be patient. Though the product first appeared on Costco shelves in Texas in April, and it has been spotted in Southern California, it hasn’t rolled out nationally—yet.

What are people saying about it?

On Reddit, shoppers are genuinely excited:

  • “I was so pumped to find this yesterday!!!” said one Redditor.
  • “Been looking for a Fairlife replacement because of cost,” said another.
  • “This is exactly what I’ve been waiting for Costco to put out! … Hope it hits my warehouse soon,” chimed in a third.

Of course, not every review has been glowing. A handful of shoppers said they noticed a different aftertaste. “This milk taste[s] awful!!! 🤮 Water[ed] down! It does not compare to Fairlife or Mootopia,” said one unhappy customer on Reddit.

And now for the moment of truth: How good of a deal is it?

This may be where Costco really wins.

The Kirkland version reportedly sells for around $10.59 to $11 for three 64-ounce cartons (1.5 gallons total). Comparable Fairlife milk often costs roughly $5 to $8 for 52 ounces (less than half a gallon) at traditional supermarkets. In other words, Costco shoppers are getting premium-style milk at a bulk-warehouse price—one of the main reasons the product is generating so much attention. And shoppers have been quick to point out the value:

  • “I love Fairlife, but it is now $6 to $8 near me and the 2% is difficult to find. If the Kirkland one tastes like the Fairlife, it will be worth the change,” said one Redditor.
  • “Pretty good. Fairlife is $5.32 for 0.4 gallon at my local grocery store,” said another.

When a product sparks this much conversation—especially in Costco circles—that usually means shoppers think they’ve found something worth stocking up on. And for people chasing more protein, lower sugar, lactose-free options or simply a better deal, Costco may have just delivered exactly what they’ve been waiting for.

About the expert

  • Edmund “Ed” McCormick is a food science consultant and an ingredient specialist whose work focuses on the chemistry of taste, texture and functional food ingredients. He is the founder, primary author, scientific voice and educator behind Cape Crystal Brands. His next book, The Food Questions America Is Asking: How Journalists and Scientists Are Redefining What We Eat, is slated for release later this year.

Why trust us

At Reader’s Digest, we’re committed to producing high-quality content by writers with expertise and experience in their field in consultation with relevant, qualified experts. We rely on reputable primary sources, including government and professional organizations and academic institutions as well as our writers’ personal experiences where appropriate. For this piece on ultra-filtered milk, Jo Ann Ligouri tapped her experience as a journalist and food blogger to ensure all information is accurate and beneficial to readers. We verify all facts and data, back them with credible sourcing and revisit them over time to ensure they remain accurate and up to date. Read more about our team, our contributors and our editorial policies.

articles.title: 13 Readers Share the Moments That Make Them Feel the Most American
articles.content:
你是否记得这样一个时刻——那种无可置疑的美国感？也许是在棒球场起立唱国歌时，也许是在国庆日观看烟花时，也许是仰望美国国旗时感受到了某种意料之外的情愫。无论是什么，那一刻都以一种出人意料的深刻方式触动了你。

我们邀请读者分享那些让他们感受到爱国情怀涌动的时刻，他们的回答令人动容，而且细节出人意料地具体。有些发生在人生重要节点，有些则在完全平凡的日子里不期而至。

让我们一起走进这些或大或小的时刻，思考身为美国人意味着什么。

**庭院里飘扬的国旗让我想起在陆军服役期间于世界各地军事基地看到的旗帜。我想到自己为保卫美国及其他寻求支持的国家的自由做出了贡献。我也为自己的匈牙利妻子成为美国公民感到自豪，这样她就能享受同样的自由，向国旗宣誓效忠。这面旗帜让我想起那些曾经服役、正在服役和将要服役的人们。**——布鲁斯·米泽尔，田纳西州西摩

**去迪士尼乐园或华特迪士尼世界让我感到非常美国化。这一切都源于有远见的沃特·迪士尼的规划，他凭借坚韧不拔的毅力使之成为现实，尽管当年他带着一只行李箱和一个梦想来到加利福尼亚州时几乎一无所有。然而，他创办了世界上最具辨识度的公司之一。他的故事就像一个在美国才可能实现的童话。**——黛安·施罗德，俄克拉荷马州诺曼

**我的祖父是威尔士后裔的煤矿工人，在宾夕法尼亚州生活和工作。他用拆除旧房子作为报酬获得的木材，在波科诺山脉的埃尔克湖边建造了一栋避暑小屋。至今我仍深感幸运，每年夏天都能在这间小屋里度假。我的孙女是第六代享受他劳动成果的人。**——辛迪·汉森，新泽西州托姆斯里弗

**我15岁时作为难民来到美国。在美国生活多年后，第一次回访越南时百感交集——我认出了故土，但它已不再是我的家。我无法像从前那样感受到归属感，也意识到美国改变了我多少。直到那时我才明白：我现在是美国人了。当我在洛杉矶机场通过海关时，一位工作人员说：“欢迎回家。”我眼眶湿润，终于意识到美国真的是我的家。**——徐薇，加利福尼亚州方廷瓦利

**街头天文是一种与陌生人分享宇宙的方式，就像在街头弹吉他，只是少了打开琴盒的环节。在晴朗的夜晚，我在城市公园架设好望远镜，表演就开始了。“你以前用望远镜看过土星吗？”我会问路过的陌生人，邀请他们来一窥究竟。“我能看到光环了！和照片里一模一样！”人们惊喜地喊道。“如果没喊出声，说明你还没看到。”我说。“你是哪里人？”他们告诉我，各种口音和地名让我仿佛随着望远镜在太空中漫游，环游世界。这让我感到热情、好奇、勇敢。分享宇宙的时刻，是我感到最美国化的时刻。继续仰望星空吧。**——乔希·厄班，弗吉尼亚州拉斯堡

**奥斯卡颁奖典礼或超级碗这样盛大的活动让我感到非常美国化。我们有天赋和能力将这里的任何事情变成全球参与的盛事。我们是一群坚韧不拔的人，善于把握机会去创造、成功和获胜。**——乔安娜·格利森，蒙大拿州卡利斯佩尔

**我和妻子在科罗拉多州度假时，两个路过的男人笑着说：“太棒了不是吗？”我们完全不知道他们在说什么，他们告诉我们最高法院刚刚在联邦层面将同性婚姻合法化了。我们几年前在加利福尼亚州合法化时就结婚了，但为了应对现实，我们常常开玩笑说，当我们跨越州界时会失去1138项权利。但那一天，我们真正感受到了被接纳为美国大家庭的一员。**——丽莎·米勒，加利福尼亚州波托拉

**“9·11”事件几周后，我回到曼哈顿下城工作。公司发放了国旗胸针，我自豪地佩戴着。当我环顾地铁车厢，几乎每个人都戴着类似的东西。纽约市有很多元化，但在那一刻，我们都是美国人，彼此对视，点头致意。我当然不怀念那场灾难及其后果，但我确实有时会怀念那种团结。**——约翰·艾伯特，纽约州德波西特

**赛场上的国旗、军乐队、飞机编队飞行表演和国歌都能激发爱国情怀。但当我看到电视新闻中移民宣誓成为新公民时，我感到最美国化。听到他们背诵效忠誓词，意识到他们为此付出了多少努力，我屏住了呼吸。**——安妮·雷蒂希，俄亥俄州佩里斯堡

**这听起来可能有些矛盾，但当我不同意美国的政策或行动时，我感到最美国。让我解释一下。我是越战老兵，幸运地健康回国。但我永远不能忘记58000名美国人失去了生命，我并不总是支持我们在海外的行动。我为能够公开表达与政府的不同意见感到自豪和欣慰，当我出于关心和爱批评我的国家时，我感到最美国。**——埃德·巴雷特，堪萨斯州普拉特

**在美国寄信本身就是一个微小的奇迹。我只需付不到一美元，就有人会将我的信亲手送到千里之外的祖母手中。有人——一个美国同胞——几乎每天都在努力将信件送到我家门口？这是一个被忽视的福祉。**——麦迪逊·陈，马萨诸塞州沃特敦

**我和妻子实现了毕生梦想，参加了《国家地理》组织的博茨瓦纳之旅。我们的许多导游是当地的年轻人或有前途的学生，他们对贝拉克·奥巴马不久前的就职典礼表达了钦佩。他们曾以为这个世界只由通过武力而非法律夺取权力的人统治，但对领导层更替竟然没有发生冲突或军事干预感到惊讶。这次旅行令人难忘，原因多方面。**——约翰·米勒，明尼苏达州伯米吉
articles.summary:
Can you remember a moment when you felt unmistakably American? Maybe it was standing for the national anthem at a baseball game, watching fireworks on the 4th of July or looking up at the American flag and feeling something you didn’t expect. Whatever it was, it hit you in a surprisingly profound way.

We asked readers to share memories of when they felt that patriotic surge, and their answers were touching and surprisingly specific. Some happened during major life milestones, while others came in the middle of totally ordinary days.

Read on for the moments, big and small, that will make you think about what it means to be an American.

Get Reader’s Digest’s Read Up newsletter for more true stories, fun facts, humor, cleaning, travel and tech all week long.

Basking in old glory

The flag flying in my yard reminds me of those I saw at military posts all over the world while serving in the Army. I think of helping to preserve our freedoms in the United States and other countries seeking support. I’m also proud of my Hungarian wife’s becoming an American citizen so she could enjoy the same freedoms and pledge her allegiance to the flag too. It reminds me of those who have served, serve today and will serve. —Bruce Mizell, Seymour, Tennessee

Where dreams come true

Visiting Disney World or Disneyland feels quite American to me. It was all the plan of visionary Walt Disney, who had the tenacity and grit to make it happen even though he had little more than a suitcase and a dream when he ventured to California. Still, he started one of the most recognizable companies in the world. His story feels like a fairy tale most possible in America. —Diane Schroeder, Norman, Oklahoma

Building a legacy

My grandfather was a coal miner of Welsh descent, living and working in Pennsylvania. He built a summer cottage at Elk Lake in the Pocono Mountains with lumber he got to keep as payment for tearing down an old house. To this day, I feel blessed to stay at the cottage every summer. My granddaughter is the sixth generation to enjoy the fruits of his labor. —Cindy Hansen, Toms River, New Jersey

Home sweet home

I came to the U.S. from Vietnam as a refugee when I was 15. After years in the States, visiting Vietnam for the first time was emotional—I recognized my homeland, but it was no longer my home. I didn’t feel that I belonged as I once did and was reminded of how much the U.S. had changed me. That’s when I understood: I’m an American now. When I cleared customs at LAX, an agent said, “Welcome home.” Tearing up, I realized, America really is my home. —Thuy Vu, Fountain Valley, California

Sharing the heavens

Sidewalk astronomy is a way to share the universe with strangers. It’s like playing guitar on the street, minus the open case. I set up my telescope in a city park on a clear night and the show begins. “Have you seen Saturn through a telescope before?” I’ll ask passing strangers, inviting them to take a look. “I can see the rings! It looks like the photos!” people exclaim with delight. “If you’re not yelling, you’re not seeing it,” I say. “So, where are you from?” They tell me, in a tapestry of accents and places that take me around the globe as we wander across the sky with my telescope. It makes me feel hospitable, curious, bold. Sharing the universe is when I feel most American. Keep looking up. —Josh Urban, Rustburg, Virginia

Moments of triumph

Over-the-top events like the Academy Awards or the Super Bowl feel very American. We have the gift and ability to turn anything here into a moment of global participation. We’re a scrappy group, and we take advantage of opportunities to create, succeed and win. —Joanna Gleason, Kalispell, Montana

States and unions

My wife and I were vacationing in Colorado when two men walking by said, “Isn’t it great?” with huge smiles. We had no idea what they were talking about, and they told us the Supreme Court had just legalized same-sex marriage on the federal level. We’d married years earlier when it became legal for us in California, but to cope, we often joked about the 1,138 rights we’d lose when we crossed state lines. But that day, we truly felt welcomed into the fold of America. —Lisa Miller, Portola, California

Standing tall, together

A few weeks after 9/11, I returned to work in lower Manhattan. My employer issued flag lapels, which I proudly wore. As I looked around my subway car, I realized almost everyone wore something similar. There’s lots of diversity in New York City, but in that moment, we were all just Americans, looking at one another and nodding. I certainly don’t miss the cause or its repercussions, but I do sometimes miss the camaraderie. —John Albert, Deposit, New York

Swearing in

Flags, military bands, flyovers and “The Star-Spangled Banner” at sporting events all inspire patriotism. But I feel the most American when I see immigrants being sworn in as new citizens on TV news broadcasts. Hearing the Pledge of Allegiance and acknowledging the hard work it took for them to reach that moment takes my breath away. —Anne Rettig, Perrysburg, Ohio

Our great debates

It sounds counterintuitive, but I feel most American when I disagree with U.S. policies and actions. Let me explain. I’m a Vietnam War veteran fortunate enough to have returned home healthy. But I can’t ever forget that 58,000 Americans died, and I don’t always support our actions overseas. I take pride and comfort in being able to openly disagree with our government, and I feel most American when I criticize my country from a place of concern and love. —Ed Barrett, Pratt, Kansas

Don’t mail it in

Sending mail as we do in the U.S. is a minor miracle. I can pay less than a dollar, and someone will hand-deliver my letter to my grandmother thousands of miles away. The idea that ­someone—a fellow American—makes the effort to reach my house nearly every day? An overlooked blessing. —Madison Chan, Watertown, Massachusetts

Gaining perspective

My wife and I fulfilled a lifelong dream of taking a National Geographic trip to Botswana. Many of our guides were young adults and promising students from the area, and they expressed admiration for Barack Obama’s then-recent inauguration. They had visualized the world as ruled only by those who took power by force rather than by law and were amazed that leadership change took place without discord or military intervention. The trip was quite memorable, for a variety of ­reasons. —John Miller, Bemidji, Minnesota

Why trust us

At Reader’s Digest, we’re committed to producing high-quality content by writers with expertise and experience in their field in consultation with relevant, qualified experts. We rely on reputable primary sources, including government and professional organizations and academic institutions as well as our writers’ personal experiences where appropriate. We verify all facts and data, back them with credible sourcing and revisit them over time to ensure they remain accurate and up to date. Read more about our team, our contributors and our editorial policies.

咖啡在煮，吐司在烤，每个州的民众已经在享用他们惯常的早餐了。在《美国美味》第八期中，我们聚焦于定义各地早晨的那些食物。我们的编辑与《家庭美食》的同事们一起收集了人们所钟爱的早餐主食，从简单易做的三明治和上学前倒的即食麦片，到撒满糖粉的温暖贝果、丰盛的goetta（德式肉肠）、热气腾腾的墨西哥卷饼和地方特色美食，这些都体现了各地如何开启新的一天。想了解更多美食、有趣事实、幽默、清洁、旅行和科技内容，请继续阅读您所在州的早餐是什么样的。咖啡的配比为蜂蜜味、金黄色的糖浆，软面包、黄油饼干和温暖的华夫饼都值得淋上这种糖浆。瓶子上的标语最能说明问题：“这东西是‘阿拉巴马州的骄傲’。”人们在费耶特县特别自豪，因为工厂为这个小镇赢得了“糖浆城”的昵称。

这些香肠由驯鹿（家养驯鹿）、牛肉和猪肉混合制成，自淘金热以来，这种香肠就与酸面包一起成为阿拉斯加的主食，当时淘金者为了保持酸面团发酵，将酸面团放在身边。在那个边疆地区很难获得酵母。

软玉米饼包裹着调味的土豆、炒鸡蛋和融化的奶酪，形成一个便携、高蛋白的包裹，再用辛辣的墨西哥香肠、辣椒和新鲜的萨尔萨酱调味，使这道菜更加开胃。有些人喜欢他们的酱汁薄而顺滑；有些人则更喜欢几乎像布丁一样浓稠的口感。但无论如何，当用“sopping chocolate”（一种用勺子盛放的巧克力酱）时，你一定会想把每滴美味都喝光。加利福尼亚州大约90%的牛油果被收获，这使得多谷物吐司上的牛油果成为健康美味的早餐。

这种咖啡蛋糕通常以蛋奶和水果（如苹果、桃子、大黄或李子）为馅，甜得让人在享用时往往选择喝黑咖啡。虽然许多南方州都以饼干和（香肠）肉汁为特色，但在田纳西州，人们更喜欢在但牛奶饼干上淋上带有胡椒味的白酱。早在田纳西州黎巴嫩市开设的第一家Cracker Barrel连锁餐厅进入43个州之前，这家店每年供应2亿个饼干。早在2017年早餐塔可成为官方州早餐的年份之前，德克萨斯州的早餐就一直包裹在温暖的玉米饼里。通常的馅料包括炒鸡蛋、奶酪、培根，以及豆类或土豆。虽然Chorizo辣肠和香菜是可选的，但在德克萨斯州，辣酱是必不可少的。这些并非传统的茶点司康饼：这些犹他州的最爱是松软的油炸面团（类似于sopaipilla或炸面包），最好趁热吃，涂上黄油、蜂蜜或兼而有之的蜂蜜黄油。无论是搭配薄煎饼还是新鲜雪片（不开玩笑），在佛蒙特州，纯枫糖浆是唯一可接受的选择，该州去年生产了超过300万加仑的枫糖浆。在鸡蛋旁边或夹在松软的饼干里，弗吉尼亚州的火腿是王者。一个真正的南方主食是像牛排一样烤制，并配以红眼肉汁，这种肉汁由其自身的汤汁和浓咖啡制成。咖啡的苦味能带出火腿的烟熏味。营养学家可能有不同意见，但在给了我们星巴克的州里，一杯足够大的咖啡本身就是早餐。在这些地方，人们聚集在一起参加无限量的“荞麦餐”，这是对1859年6月大霜冻期间阿巴拉契亚人坚韧不拔的致敬。在薄煎饼中，它带来了一种焦糖味和微苦的味道，正好需要大量糖蜜或苹果黄油。如果要在中午前饮酒，你不妨把它当作一顿饭！斯康尼人喜欢他们的血腥玛丽分量十足——配芹菜、奶酪块、牛肉条、培根、泡菜、腌菜、泡豆、橄榄，甚至可能还有一个小汉堡在上面。别忘了来一杯（一小杯啤酒）。这可能是开启一天最威斯康星州的方式。没有花哨，没有装饰。只是燃料。它从在火上煮沸的粗咖啡粉开始，里面加了一点蛋壳来中和酸度，倒出前帮助沉淀。剩下的部分在铸铁锅里烹饪：煎蛋、土豆和烟熏麋鹿香肠，所有这些都在平底锅里一起滋滋作响。在Reader's Digest，我们致力于通过与相关领域的专家协商，由具有专业知识和经验的作家制作高质量的内容。我们依靠声誉良好的主要来源，包括政府和专业组织以及学术机构，以及我们作家的个人经验。我们核实所有事实和数据，用可靠的来源加以支持，并随时间推移重新审视它们，以确保它们保持准确和最新。阅读更多关于我们团队、贡献者和编辑政策的信息。文章《美味美国：每个州的最佳早餐》首次发表于Reader's Digest。
articles.summary:
The coffee is brewing, the toaster is popping, and somewhere in every state, people are already reaching for their go-to breakfasts. For Reader’s Digest’s eighth installment of America the Tasty, we zoomed in on the foods that define mornings where you live.

Our editors, along with our colleagues at Taste of Home, pulled together the breakfast staples people swear by, from easy-to-make sandwiches and cold cereal poured before school runs, to warm beignets dusted in sugar, hearty goetta, sizzling huevos rancheros and regional favorites that say a lot about how each place starts its day.

Hungry yet? Read on to see what breakfast looks like in your state.

Get Reader’s Digest’s Read Up newsletter for more food, fun facts, humor, cleaning, travel and tech all week long.

Alabama: Golden Eagle Syrup

Short stacks, buttermilk biscuits and warm waffles all deserve drizzles of this honey-flavored, honey-colored syrup. The slogan on the bottle says it best: This stuff is the “Pride of Alabama.” Folks in Fayette feel especially proud, given that the factory has earned the town the nickname Syrup City.

Alaska: Reindeer sausage

Made with a mix of meat—reindeer (domesticated caribou), beef and pork—these sausages sizzle alongside sourdough pancakes, an Alaska staple since the gold rush, when prospectors kept sourdough starter on their person to keep it alive. (Yeast was hard to come by on the frontier.)

Arizona: Breakfast burritos

Soft tortillas swaddle seasoned potatoes, scrambled eggs and melty cheese, creating a portable, protein-rich parcel made all the more eye-opening with spicy chorizo, chiles and fresh salsa.

Arkansas: Biscuits and chocolate gravy

Some like their sauce thin and silky; others prefer it almost as thick as pudding. But either way, when buttermilk biscuits are anointed with a ladleful of “sopping chocolate,” you’ll want to soak up every delicious drop.

California: Avocado toast

The Golden State harvests about 90% of American-grown avocados, which make hearty (and heart-healthy) meals when spread on multigrain toast. In a land of such agricultural abundance, you can pair avocado toast with a fruit smoothie and hit your five-a-day nice and early.

Colorado: Denver omelet

Eggs, ham, peppers, onion and sometimes cheese: You know this diner classic (also called a western omelet), but did you know it was first served between slices of bread? The “Denver sandwich” was popular in the country around 1950. Nowadays, the toast is more likely to come on the side.

Connecticut: French toast

Stars Hollow isn’t real (sorry, Gilmore Girls fans), but Connecticut has lots of quaint little towns like it—along with diners like Luke’s, where French toast was the longtime special. Nutmeggers know their namesake spice is essential to the batter, adding another layer of cozy comfort to the brunch favorite.

Delaware: Scrapple

These bricks of pork scraps, cornmeal and spices are big throughout the Mid-Atlantic, but Delaware makes the most of them. Don’t dwell on what’s inside; just focus on their taste (similar to sausage) and texture: crispy on the outside, tantalizingly tender in the center.

Florida: Guava pastries

While visitors to the Sunshine State might start their days with Mickey waffles, many residents prefer pastelitos (“little pastries”), a familiar term even among Florida’s non-Spanish speakers. These flaky, glazed Cuban delicacies come with a variety of fillings, some of them savory, but the classic is guava and cream cheese. Of course, what better to wash them down with than a glass of fresh-squeezed orange juice?

Georgia: Bacon

Before Banana Ball blew up, the Savannah Bananas’ main rival was the Macon Bacon, a summer collegiate baseball team—with a mascot called Kevin (get it?). In 2023, the city of Macon added a bacon trail, featuring 19 local eateries with recipes that celebrate the beloved breakfast side. Try all 19 (not exactly a hardship) and earn a season ticket to see the Macon Bacon play.

Hawaii: Spam musubi

The canned meat, pan-fried to perfection with a sweet soy glaze, perches atop sticky rice sprinkled with furikake (a Japanese seasoning mix), all tied together with a strip of toasted seaweed. This grab-and-go bite is the total package, ready to jump-start busy mornings and breezy beach days alike with the sweet-and-umami punch locals love.

Idaho: Hash browns

Let’s call a spud a spud: Idaho produces more potatoes than any other state. Many of them are destined for the shredder, then the fryer, before being served across the country. In fact, the 24/7 breakfast chain Waffle House sources its spuds strictly from family farmers in southeastern Idaho.

Illinois: Apple pancakes

Lines start early at legendary Chicago pancake houses Walker Bros. and Apple Villa, where the sized-to-share baked apple pancakes come with a custardy interior and a bounty of caramelized Granny Smith apples on top.

Indiana: Cinnamon rolls

Rolled by expert hands and baked to nearly the size of your head, gigantic and generously frosted cinnamon rolls are a must-eat attraction in Indiana’s Amish country, including at the region’s famous huge flea market.

Iowa: Casey’s breakfast pizza

Far from leftovers, these pies at Casey’s, the Iowa-based gas station and convenience store chain, are meant for the morning, with cheese sauce or sausage gravy on pizza crust, plus scrambled eggs, cheddar and mozzarella, topped with bacon, sausage or veggies. Grab a slice and a coffee—maybe a scotcheroo too!—and breakfast (to go) is served.

Kansas: Hash brown casserole

Expect a hearty plate in the heart of America: eggs, bacon, pancakes and—perhaps the (morning) glory of them all—hash brown casserole. The homey delicacy combines hash brown potatoes with cheese, your choice of “cream of …” soup and a crispy, buttery topping. (Cornflakes are a common choice.)

Kentucky: Breakfast Hot Brown

The standard version of this open-faced hot turkey sandwich with bacon, tomato and Mornay sauce (a cheesy bechamel) could be considered a brunch item. But many Kentucky cafes move it to breakfast territory by putting it on an English muffin or a biscuit and adding an egg or two to the equation.

Louisiana: Beignets

Big Easy mornings come with a shower of powdered sugar over hot, plump squares of fried dough that visitors and locals alike line up for. Between bites, sipping a slightly bitter chicory coffee balances the beignets’ sweetness.

Maine: Ployes

Not quite crepes, ployes (rhymes with boys) are similarly thin, griddled goodies that cook on just one side. (No flipping necessary.) With only five ingredients—buckwheat flour, wheat flour, baking powder, salt and water—they let their toppings, including other Maine specialties such as blueberries and maple syrup, be the stars of the show.

Maryland: Crab Benedict

Eggs Benedict, meet the Chesapeake Bay. The breakfast classic of poached eggs and Canadian bacon on an English muffin with hollandaise sauce gets a simple swap here: crabmeat for the Canadian bacon.

Massachusetts: Corn muffins

One of just a handful of states with an official state muffin, Massachusetts picked the corn muffin (perhaps it grew tired of all the cran­berries) in 1986. Bay Staters’ beloved Dunkin’ Donuts also deserves a mention here—although not all Dunkin’ locations carry corn muffins, many in Massachusetts do.

Michigan: Cereal

Battle Creek, Michigan, also answers to the name Cereal City. It’s where W.K. Kellogg created cornflakes and C.W. Post struck gold with Grape-Nuts, launching a long line of easy meals and enthusiastic mascots including Toucan Sam and Tony the Tiger.

Minnesota: Breakfast hot dish

Casseroles are cultural phenomena in the Land of 10,000 Lakes, where you’re more likely to hear them called hot dishes. The standard morning model has eggs, cheese and sausage with Tater Tots on top, dontcha know?

Mississippi: Grits

No single southern state is the ultimate authority on grits. But they are massive in Mississippi, where they combine with notoriously creamy Mississippi Sin dip (featuring cheddar cheese, cream cheese and sour cream) into one sumptuous and scrumptious casserole.

Missouri: Homemade Pop-Tarts

Ever since it was reported that Taylor Swift had baked homemade Pop-Tarts for Travis Kelce (and other Kansas City Chiefs linemen) to celebrate a win in the 2023–2024 season, similar toaster treats have popped up at pastry shops all over the Show-Me State.

Montana: Huckleberry pancakes

Tart-sweet huckleberries find their way into all kinds of treats here, like bear claws and the coffee cake called huckleberry buckle. Families fold freshly foraged huckleberries into pancake batter and griddle it up with pride.

Nebraska: Breakfast Runza

Fans of the fast-food brand Runza have been begging for a breakfast version of its eponymous pocket sandwich (which features ground beef and cabbage). The chain occasionally acquiesces—albeit at select stores and on specific days, often to raise money for charity. When a Lincoln location offered ham, egg and cheese Runzas one day in April 2025, the last one was gone by 9 a.m., and the store collected $1,600 in donations.

Nevada: Steak and eggs

Whether or not you’ve been up all night (what happens in Vegas …), juicy steak and eggs (“steggs” for short) with a side of crispy potatoes and toast will get you back on track for the day ahead.

New Hampshire: Corned beef hash

Irish immigrants breathed new life into dinner leftovers of boiled corned beef, cabbage, potatoes and onion by chopping and frying it all up in the morning. Some folks add beets and call it red flannel hash, a nod to the color—and to the attire of New Hampshire’s loggers.

New Jersey: Taylor ham/pork roll

The only debate in the Garden State over its favorite egg-and-cheese sandwich is what to call the meat inside it: Taylor ham to those in the north, and pork roll to those in the south.

New Mexico: Huevos rancheros

In the Land of Enchantment, breakfast comes with a question—red or green? That is, which type of Hatch chile sauce to top a plate of sunny-side-up eggs nestled on lightly fried tortillas (beans and cheese optional). On the side, a warm sopaipilla dipped in honey helps take the edge off the spice.

New York: Bagels

It’s no secret New Yorkers take pride in their bagels, often citing the softer water in New York City as the reason for their superiority. (In reality, their method of boiling the bagels before baking them makes more of a difference.) A simple schmear of cream cheese will do, but hungrier New Yorkers from Brooklyn to Buffalo might go all in and make it a “BEC” by adding bacon, egg and cheese.

North Carolina: Krispy Kreme doughnuts

As long as the Hot Now sign is lit up, indicating that a fresh batch is coming off the conveyor belt, Krispy Kreme can be breakfast, lunch or dinner to Tar Heels. But dunking an Original Glazed into a cup of coffee is a surefire start to a great day.

North Dakota: Caramel rolls

Also dubbed the Dakota Roll, this ooey-gooey goodie starts with a pan of pillowy-soft cinnamon rolls baked in a pool of buttery caramel (said with two syllables here: CAR-mull). After cooling a smidge, the whole shebang is inverted so the sweet stuff seeps into every swirl.

Ohio: Goetta

GET-uh (an admittedly Americanized pronunciation) goes back to German immigrants adapting their own version of grützwurst, a grain-based sausage. The combination of pork, spices and oats (which are right at home in Ohio—Quaker Oats got its start in Akron) has become a Buckeye staple. It’s often sliced into patties, pan-fried till crispy and served alongside runny eggs.

Oklahoma: Chicken-fried steak

Sooners salivate over chicken-fried steak day and night, so it’s up to the rest of the meal to make it feel like breakfast. Handy, then, that Oklahoma officials designated an entire state meal. It stars chicken-fried steak, of course, but also includes sides that work well in the a.m.: grits, biscuits and strawberries.

Oregon: Toast with marionberry jam

The deep purple marionberry is an Oregon original: Developed in the state in the 1940s and grown almost exclusively in the Willamette Valley, it is sweeter than a blackberry and makes a juicy jam.

Pennsylvania: Chicken and waffles

Far from the fried fare you find in the South, Pennsylvania Dutch chicken and waffles features a creamy chicken stew spooned over waffles that are often made in a grill pan, not an electric waffle maker. (This is Amish country, after all.)

Rhode Island: Jonnycakes

Our smallest state is deeply divided over these cornmeal pancakes, right down to how to spell them. (Some prefer johnnycake or even journeycake.) The biggest dispute, however, is over how to make them: In South County, for example, the cornmeal is combined with boiling water, and the cakes come out thick. In Newport County, it’s mixed with cold milk for a thinner result.

South Carolina: Shrimp and grits

Also called breakfast shrimp, this dish is a staple of the South, combining creamy grits with creek-caught shrimp from the South Carolina Lowcountry.

South Dakota: Kuchen

Coffee and kuchen (KOO-ken, German for “cake”) is a combo South Dakotans embrace from daybreak to dessert. This coffee cake is usually filled with custard and fruit (such as apples, peaches, rhubarb or prunes), and it’s often so sweet that many who partake in the ritual keep their coffee black.

Tennessee: Biscuits and gravy

Sure, lots of southern states lay claim to biscuits and (sausage) gravy. But in Tennessee, Volunteers readily pour the peppery white sauce over buttermilk biscuits. Cracker Barrel, which first opened in Lebanon, Tennessee, before spreading into 43 more states, serves 200 million biscuits each year.

Texas: Breakfast tacos

Lone Star mornings have come wrapped in warm tortillas since long before 2017, when breakfast tacos became the official state breakfast. The usual fillings include soft-scrambled eggs, cheese, bacon, and beans or potatoes. Chorizo and cilantro may be optional, but in Texas, hot sauce is not.

Utah: Utah scone

These scones aren’t the traditional teatime variety: These Utah favorites are fluffy pillows of deep-fried dough (similar to sopaipilla or fry bread), best served warm and slathered with butter, honey or—the best of both worlds—honey butter.

Vermont: Maple syrup

Whatever the vehicle for it, be it flapjacks or fresh snow (not kidding), pure maple syrup is the only acceptable option in Vermont, which produces the most of it by a mile—more than 3 million gallons last year.

Virginia: Country ham

Nestled next to eggs or tucked into ­tender biscuits, country ham is king in the commonwealth. One true southern staple sees it grilled like steak and served with red-eye gravy made from its own drippings and strong coffee. (The bitterness of the coffee brings out the smoky flavor of the ham.)

Washington: Coffee

Nutritionists may disagree, but in the state that gave us Starbucks, a cup of coffee (when large enough) can be breakfast in and of itself.

West Virginia: Buckwheat cakes

In these parts, folks gather for all-you-can-eat “buckwheat feeds,” an ode to Appalachian resilience during the Big Frost of June 1859, when early settlers turned to hardy buckwheat after their other crops failed. In pancakes, it lends a toasty, slightly bitter note that’s just begging for a good douse of sorghum syrup or apple butter.

Wisconsin: Bloody Mary

If you’re going to drink before noon, you might as well make a meal out of it! Sconnies famously like their Bloody Marys bountiful—with celery, cheese curds, beef sticks, bacon, pickles, dilly beans, olives and maybe even a li’l burger on top. Don’t forget the sidecar (a small beer served alongside). It just might be the most Wisconsin way to start the day.

Wyoming: Cowboy breakfast

No fuss. No frills. Just fuel. It starts with coarse coffee grounds simmered in a pot over a fire, with a little eggshell in it to cut the acidity and help the grounds settle before pouring. The rest cooks in cast iron: fried eggs, potatoes and smoky elk sausage, all sizzling together in the skillet.

Why trust us

At Reader’s Digest, we’re committed to producing high-quality content by writers with expertise and experience in their field in consultation with relevant, qualified experts. We rely on reputable primary sources, including government and professional organizations and academic institutions as well as our writers’ personal experiences where appropriate. We verify all facts and data, back them with credible sourcing and revisit them over time to ensure they remain accurate and up to date. Read more about our team, our contributors and our editorial policies.

articles.title: Forget Destination Weddings—The Latest Travel Trend Extends to Divorce Parties, Midlife Crises and Even Funerals
articles.content:
我表妹莎伦是美国人，在西班牙塞维利亚举行目的地婚礼前，她向当地花商订购了六束"花束"（ramos）送给伴娘们。结果花商听成了"船桨"（remos），送来了六把系着丝带的木桨。目的地婚礼的趣事往往在于它们引发的各种意外。比如托斯卡纳的那场婚礼，准新娘在Reddit上分享说，她主动提出帮一位经济困难的亲戚支付机票费用。这本是好意，但消息传开后，很快就有十几个亲戚及其随行人员也期望这对新人报销旅费。于是那对恋人最终选择了私奔。如今，目的地庆祝活动已不仅限于婚礼。美国汽车协会最近的一项调查显示，76%计划在2026年旅行的美国人表示，他们会根据人生里程碑事件来规划行程，比如毕业典礼和周年纪念日。考虑到这一点，你可以考虑用目的地庆祝活动来纪念人生中同样值得护照相伴的时刻。

孟菲斯的艾莉森在40岁离婚时，朋友们没有送花，而是给了她飞往迈阿密的往返机票。"我们称之为我的'单身之旅'，"她在Reddit上写道。六位女士抵达酒店时，工作人员为她戴上了"刚离婚"的皇冠和肩带。她们用"赡养马提尼"干杯，Shot杯上印着"来一杯，我刚解除了婚约"的标语。"起初感觉有点傻，但随后就像是新的开始，"艾莉森说，"特别是当礼宾员递给我一个印有枷锁图案的纸杯蛋糕，我们一直狂欢到凌晨2点。"果然，电子邀请平台Evite的数据显示，离婚派对邀请函已飙升至历史新高。一些酒店甚至推出了"离婚派对套餐"，包括水疗护理和分手歌单，曲目包括《我会活下去》《自从你走后》和《真相伤人》等。"举办离婚派对不仅是一种仪式，用来承认人生篇章的结束，"马里兰州洛约拉大学的社会心理学家特蕾莎·迪多纳托表示，"这也反映了一个人拥抱单身新身份的一种方式，与前任分离。"需要派对纪念品吗？沃尔玛出售"一段错误的终结"气球和"我结了、我离了、结束了"横幅。Etsy上有一款蜡烛名为"闻起来像刚签好的离婚协议书"。亚马逊则有"再见了，错误的先生"贴纸和写着"我从来不喜欢他"的临时纹身。

亚利桑那州图森市的安德烈亚为了实现父亲的遗愿，研究了如何在夏威夷撒放骨灰。她发现夏威夷骨灰撒放公司提供私人纪念航游，即日落巡航。当租用的船在瓦胡岛附近缓缓漂流时，一位夏威夷卡胡（牧师）开始轻声吟唱，他的助手举起海螺吹响悠长回响的号角。家人撒下可降解的花环和花瓣，轻轻漂浮在水面上，牧师放飞了帝王蝶。甲板上的日落晚餐为这一天画上句号。"他们安排好了一切，从餐饮到预订尤克里里琴师，"安德烈亚在Tripadvisor上回忆道。皇家加勒比等邮轮公司现在也悄悄提供海葬服务，在甲板上举行私人仪式——前提是你携带所需文件（死亡证明和火化证明），船只距离海岸至少3海里（根据美国环保署规定），骨灰装在可降解的骨灰盒中抛入海中。实际上不允许直接撒骨灰，因为其他乘客不会希望在他们的迈泰鸡尾酒里看到爷爷的骨灰飘进来。佛罗里达州阿波帕市的德·维拉维拉用这种方式送别了丈夫，她发现过程比预期顺利。一位船员带她的小团体到下层甲板，当她将骨灰盒抛入船尾航迹时，阳光在水面上闪闪发光。两天后，她在邮轮评论网上发帖说，她收到了一份证书，标注了海葬的确切经纬度——一份宁静告别的温馨纪念。和她一样的人不在少数。根据美国殡葬主管协会的数据，近六成美国人曾在非传统场所参加葬礼。有些人选择将亲人的骨灰撒在约塞米蒂国家公园（需要许可证）；有些人在新奥尔良街头以爵士葬礼游行的方式送别亲人。文化人类学家、《我死去的那一天》作者安妮塔·汉尼格表示，她将目的地告别视为"一个更大趋势的一部分……家庭将传统葬礼仪式转变为更加个性化、量身定制的方式，以符合逝者的精神"。

说到最特别的告别方式，斯莱特里航天纪念公司便是一个典范。该公司在加利福尼亚州范登堡太空军基地发射骨灰。当《星际迷航》中"斯科蒂"扮演者詹姆斯·杜汉的骨灰被送入轨道时，他的遗孀温迪·杜汉形容这段经历出人意料地令人欣慰："你完全能感受到他们正在某个地方欢呼，'太棒了！'"

一段原本浪漫的蜜月很快变得尴尬——一位新娘在新度假村大堂发现了一个意想不到的惊喜：丈夫最好的朋友和妻子。正如她在Reddit上解释的那样，她的丈夫瞒着她邀请了他们。蜜月瞬间变成团体旅行——共同游览、集体晚餐，以及"零亲密"。当愤怒的新娘终于与懵懂的新郎对峙时，他耸耸肩说他们可以"改天"再来一次私人旅行。"蜜月加朋友"——把一生一次的浪漫 getaway变成一场旅行马戏团——已变得越来越普遍。总部位于英国的婚礼礼品清单服务公司Prezola的调查显示，14%的订婚情侣考虑这个想法。一些新婚夫妇正在放弃曾经神圣的蜜月，因为现在许多情侣婚前就同居和旅行。他们将共享蜜月视为延长婚礼庆祝的一种方式，一场漫长的幸福永远派对。关系专家、《让婚姻从好到伟大的五个简单步骤》作者特蕾·奥尔布赫博士说，从历史上看，"蜜月的真正目的或目标是让夫妻建立联系，共度美好时光——只有两个人。蜜月是建立感情、提醒彼此当初为什么结婚的时刻。"但她说，如今的夫妇"倾向于让庆祝的快乐持续更久。在婚礼上投入了这么多工作、时间和金钱之后，他们希望与每个人的庆祝活动继续下去。"

当然，邀请朋友或亲戚参加夫妻 getaway往往会升级——无论贫富——到明显更糟。团体蜜月据报道经常适得其反，因为朋友们派对到很晚（迫使夫妻在朋友和彼此之间做出选择），亲戚夺走控制权，或者新婚夫妇假装食物中毒只是为了获得一些独处时间。然而吸引力依然存在。由于隐私从宾客名单中被剔除，"新婚"不再意味着"请勿打扰"。

在TikTok上，一位名叫莉齐的年轻女性分享了她用机票给母亲庆生的惊喜时刻——目的地是布加勒斯特，这个想法源于听到妈妈说想去那里。莉齐期待看到喜悦的泪水。相反，她的母亲只是看起来很困惑。原来她妈妈实际上说的是想"预约休息"，而不是去国外度假。但 вместо того, чтобы quiet weekend at home, she was suddenly packing for Romania. 目的地生日庆祝活动在理论上听起来很棒。你去一个梦幻的地方，为又一年干杯，在社交媒体上发布照片让所有其他朋友羡慕。有什么可能出错呢？（除了所有事情。）

据《人物》杂志报道，一位匿名女性40岁生日计划在她要求朋友们参加每对夫妇花费超过5000美元的加勒比海一周假期后适得其反。当团队拒绝为这份特权申请第二抵押贷款时，她对他们不理不睬。优雅地度过40岁就这么泡汤了。如果你收到的庆祝活动邀请超出预算，个人理财专家、《破产千禧一代谈钱》作者艾rin Lowry建议："用赞美三明治法。先给予肯定，说明你不能去并给出原因，然后以替代方案结束。可以这样说：'我绝对想和你一起庆祝，但很遗憾旅行不在我目前的预算内。等你回来后我们一起喝一杯或晚餐，你可以告诉我所有关于这次旅行的事情！'"当然，不是每个人都能理解这个建议。据《人物》杂志报道，另一位女性建议在23岁生日时去亚洲旅行，但当她的朋友们觉得太贵时，他们为同一个周末计划了一次更便宜的 getaway——没有邀请她。

啊，中年——一个因意识到太多生日会杀死你而被引发的自我反省时刻。45岁时，退休海军老兵尼克·库什没有购买闪亮的跑车，这是典型的中年危机冲动消费。相反，他买了保暖手套和北极靴。几个月后，他与一群退伍军人在距离南极洲690英里的联合冰川上空跳伞，他们的任务是七天内完成七大洲的跳伞（他们最终在六天六小时内完成，创下新的世界纪录）。南极后勤探险公司组织了团队的第一次跳跃（该探险公司也提供一周套餐，包括在极地高原的帐篷露营，对大多数冒险者来说，价格高达36000美元）。零下25度的风寒让任何关于衰老的遗憾都冻住了。"世界上只有少数人能在南极洲上空跳伞，"库什说，"我最大的恐惧不是死亡。而是85岁时被困在躺椅上。"

库什和他的队友们的冒险为"荣誉褶皱"组织筹款，这是一个为阵亡或残疾军人的家庭提供奖学金的慈善机构。"从军队退役后，一切戛然而止，"库什说，"你必须找到新的使命。我意识到我仍然可以活出一个精彩的故事，这就是我正在努力做的事情。"

"中年时身体出现衰老的早期迹象可能会唤醒人们开始实现遗愿清单的欲望，"布兰代斯大学心理学教授玛吉·拉赫曼说。事实上，市场研究公司Grand View Research报告称，50多岁的旅行者现在占冒险旅游市场的26%。冒险公司正在利用中年人的渴望，向渴望证明自己仍然有能力的旅行者推销高风险、高预算的逃离——外加止痛药和热敷贴。一家澳大利亚公司干脆就叫"中年冒险"，经营穿越蒙古、印度和尼泊尔的长途摩托车之旅，定位为40至70岁、有时间、金钱和欲望走出常规的人们的难忘体验。

如今，美食爱好者前往的地方远不止纳帕谷品酒、肯塔基州波本朝圣和葡萄牙橄榄油品尝。"皮克尔茨堡绝对值得一生参加一次，"马里兰州汉诺威的洛里说，她和三个朋友参加了这个在匹兹堡举办的多日泡菜节。活动包括泡菜冰淇淋、泡菜啤酒、泡菜主题圣诞装饰品、吃泡菜比赛和捞泡菜游戏。洛里和她的团队品尝了泡菜玛格丽塔、炸泡菜和泡菜软糖——酸、咸、令人困惑的混合体验。参与者还可以骑在一只巨大的泡菜形气球下的机械泡菜上。一位YouTube评论者称皮克尔茨堡是"我永远会珍藏的体验"。

"我们所吃所喝能带来快乐，"康奈尔大学彼得与斯蒂芬妮·诺兰酒店管理学院的资深讲师谢丽尔·斯坦利说。"旅行让我们能够扩展对一个地方、食物或饮品的认识，或者去见一位奶酪制造商或清酒生产者，通过创造持久的回忆能将这种快乐提升到更高层次。"在夏威夷威基基，夏威夷人最爱的罐头肉斯帕姆节以斯帕姆玉米饼、斯帕姆寿司和斯帕姆馄饨庆祝，吸引数万忠实粉丝。"如果你在寻找创意，这里就是了，"弗吉尼亚州费尔法克斯的蒂芙尼在Yelp上评论道。



“我是一名美食探索者，所以我想尝试所有独特的午餐肉产品——我承认，大部分我都不太喜欢，”她坦言，不过她也承认午餐肉惠灵顿“还挺不错的”。

有些品尝活动则完全是大胆尝试。缅因州威斯卡西特的缅因品尝中心每年举办的“可食用昆虫可持续晚餐”以鸡尾酒时段开场，提供以烤龙舌兰虫碎装饰的瓦哈卡玛格丽塔。客人吃的不是正式的开胃菜，而是烤蝗虫和烘烤蟋蟀。四道菜的正餐包括黄粉虫饺子配甲虫辣椒脆，最后以昆虫糖果作为派对礼物，包括蝎子棒棒糖。南波特兰的萨姆·查托说，蚂蚁点缀的玉米面包“看起来像罂粟籽”。真正的考验来了。“配有甲虫和黄粉虫的塔可饼在视觉上令人望而生畏，”他说。“你绝对不会弄错自己在吃虫子。”味道如何？“很棒，”查托说。“它们可能增加了一点脆感，但事实上，脆感很快就消失了。”

归根结底，这不仅仅是味道的问题，而是场所的问题——以及你愿意走多远。在《读者文摘》，我们致力于由具有专业领域知识和经验的作家创作高质量内容，并咨询相关合格专家。我们依靠政府、专业机构和学术机构等可靠的一手资料，以及作者的个人经历（如适用）。我们验证所有事实和数据，用可靠来源支撑，并在时间推移中重新审视，确保其保持准确和最新。了解更多关于我们团队、贡献者和编辑政策的信息。这篇帖子《忘掉目的地婚礼——最新旅游趋势延伸至离婚派对、中年危机甚至葬礼》首次发表于《读者文摘》。
articles.summary:
Before her destination wedding in Seville, Spain, my cousinSharon, an American, ordered six ramos (“bouquets”) for her bridesmaids. The local florist, hearing remos, delivered six wooden oars tied with ribbon.

Half the memories of destination weddings are the disasters they spawn. Take the Tuscany destination wedding where the bride-to-be shared on Reddit that she had offered to help a struggling relative with airfare. It was a nice gesture, but unfortunately, word spread, and soon a dozen family members and their plus-ones also expected the couple to pay for their travel. And that’s when the lovebirds decided to elope.

These days, destination celebrations aren’t limited to nuptials. A recent AAA survey found that 76% of Americans planning trips in 2026 say they’ll base them on life milestones like graduations and anniversaries. With that in mind, you might consider these destination celebrations for some of life’s equally passport-worthy occasions.

Get Reader’s Digest’s Read Up newsletter for more true stories, fun facts, humor, cleaning, travel and tech all week long.

Destination divorce party

When Alison of Memphis, Tennessee, finalized her divorce at age 40, her friends didn’t send flowers. Instead, they handed her round-trip airline tickets to Miami. “We called it my Single­moon,” she wrote on Reddit.

The six women arrived at their hotel, where the staff greeted Alison with a “Just Divorced!” tiara and sash. They toasted with “Alimony-tinis” and shot glasses printed with the slogan “Take a Shot, I Just Untied the Knot.”

“It felt silly at first, but then like a fresh start,” Alison says. “Especially when the concierge handed me a cupcake with a ball and chain on it, and we went clubbing until 2 a.m.”

Sure enough, Evite data shows that divorce party invitations have soared to a record high. Some hotels advertise “divorce party packages” with spa treatments and breakup playlists including songs like “I Will Survive,” “Since U Been Gone” and “Truth Hurts.”

“The act of throwing a divorce party may not only serve as a ritual act to acknowledge the end of a chapter,” says social psychologist Theresa DiDonato of Loyola University Maryland. “It could also reflect a way to embrace a new identity as a single person, separate from the ex.”

Need party favors? Walmart sells “The End of an Error” balloons and “I Do, I Did, I’m Done” banners. Etsy offers a candle labeled “Smells Like Freshly Signed Divorce Papers.” And Amazon features stickers printed with “So Long, Mr. Wrong” and temporary tattoos that say “I Never Liked Him.”

Destination funerals

To honor her father’s final request, Andrea from Tucson, Arizona, re­searched ways to scatter his ashes in Hawaii. She discovered that Hawaii Ash Scatterings offers private memorial voyages in the form of sunset cruises. As the small chartered boat drifted off Oahu, a Hawaiian kahu (“minister”) began a soft chant while his assistant lifted a conch shell to his lips and blew a long, echoing blast. Family members cast biodegradable leis and flower petals that floated gently on the water, and the kahu released monarch butterflies. A sunset dinner on deck completed the evening.

“They took care of everything, from food and drinks to booking a ukulele player,” Andrea recalled on Tripadvisor.

Cruise lines like Royal Caribbean now quietly offer sea burials with private ceremonies on deck—provided you bring the required paperwork (death certificate and cremation certificate), the ship is at least 3 nautical miles from shore (per EPA regulations) and the ashes are contained in a biodegradable urn that is tossed into the sea. No literal scattering of ashes is allowed, as other cruise guests frown on Grandpa’s ashes fluttering into their mai tais.

Doreen de Villalvilla from Apopka, Florida, honored her husband this way and found the process smoother than she expected. A ship’s officer led her small group to a lower deck, where she tossed the urn into the wake as sunlight shimmered on the water. Two days later, she posted on Cruise Critic that she received a certificate marking the exact longitude and latitude of the burial at sea—a touching memento of a serene farewell.

She is not alone. Nearly 6 in 10 Americans have attended a funeral in an unusual setting, according to the National Funeral Directors Association. Some choose to scatter a loved one’s ashes in Yosemite National Park (permit required); others march to the cemetery in a jazz funeral procession through the streets of New Orleans. Cultural anthropologist Anita Hannig, author of The Day I Die, says she sees destination farewells as “part of a larger trend … of families transforming traditional funeral rites into something much more personalized and individually curated to match the spirit of the departed.”

Which brings us to this stellar goodbye. Celestis Memorial Spaceflights hosts launches of cremated remains at Vandenberg Space Force Base in California. When the company blasted the ashes of Star Trek’s James “Scotty” Doohan into orbit, his widow, Wende Doohan, described the experience as surprisingly joyful: “You just know they are somewhere yelling, ‘Yahoo!’ ”

Destination buddymoon

A tropical honeymoon turned awkward quickly when one newlywed discovered an unexpected surprise waiting in the resort lobby: her husband’s best friend and his wife. As she explainedon Reddit, her husband had secretly invited them. The honeymoon quickly morphed into a group trip—shared excursions, group dinners and “zero intimacy.” When the angry bride finally confronted her oblivious groom, he shrugged and suggested they could take a private trip “another time.”

The “buddymoon”—turning a romantic, once-in-a-lifetime getaway into a traveling circus—has become increasingly common. A survey conducted by Prezola, a U.K.-based wedding gift list service, showed that 14% of engaged couples consider the idea. Some newlyweds are shunning the once-sacred honeymoon because so many couples now live and travel together before marriage. They see their shared honeymoon as a way to extend the wedding celebration into one long, happily ever after-party.

Historically, “the real purpose or goal of a honeymoon is for the couple to connect and spend quality time—alone,” says relationship expert Terri Orbuch, PhD, author of 5 Simple Steps to Take Your Marriage from Good to Great. “The honeymoon is the time to bond and remind each other why they married in the first place.” But today’s couples, she says, “are oriented toward making the celebratory fun last longer. After putting so much work, time and money into the wedding, they want the celebration with everyone to keep on going.”

Of course, inviting friends or relatives to join a conjugal escape tends to escalate “for better or worse” to decidedly worse. Group honeymoons reportedly often backfire when friends party late (forcing the couple to choose their posse or each other), relatives wrest control, or the newlyweds pretend to have food poisoning just to get some time alone. Yet the appeal persists. And with privacy cut from the guest list, “just married” no longer means “do not disturb.”

Destination birthday(gone wrong)

On TikTok, a young woman named Lizzy shared a video of the moment she surprised her mother with airline tickets to Bucharest for her birthday, an idea sparked by hearing her mom say she wanted to go there. Lizzy expected tears of joy. Instead, her mother just looked confused. Turns out her mom had actually said she wanted to “book a rest,” not a foreign vacation. But instead of a quiet weekend at home, she was suddenly packing for Romania.

Destination birthday celebrations sound great in theory. You travel somewhere fabulous, toast another trip around the sun and post pictures on social media to make all your other friends jealous. What could possibly go wrong? (Besides everything.)

According to People, one anonymous woman’s 40th birthday plans backfired after she asked her friends to join her for a weeklong Caribbean vacation costing more than $5,000 per couple. When the group refused to take out second mortgages for the privilege, she gave them the silent treatment. So much for turning 40 gracefully.

If you’re invited to a celebration outside your budget, personal finance expert Erin Lowry, author of Broke Millennial Talks Money, suggests: “Go with the compliment sandwich. Start with a positive, state that you can’t go and include a reason why, then end with an alternative. It could look like this: ‘I absolutely want to celebrate with you, but unfortunately taking a trip just isn’t in my budget right now. Let’s do happy hour or dinner when you get back and you can tell me all about the trip!’ ”

Of course, not everyone gets the memo. People reports that another woman suggested a trip to Asia for her 23rd birthday, but when her friends found that too expensive, they planned a cheaper getaway for the same ­weekend—without her.

Destination midlife crisis trip

Ah, middle age—a time of self-reflection prompted by the realization that too many birthdays can kill you.

At age 45, retired Navy veteran Nick Kush didn’t buy a shiny sports car, the standard midlife crisis splurge. Instead, he bought thermal gloves and Arctic boots. A few months later, he was skydiving over Union Glacier, 690 miles from the South Pole, with a team of ­fellow veterans on a mission to free-fall over all seven continents in seven days (and they wound up doing it in six days and six hours, a new world record).

Antarctic Logistics & Expeditions organized the team’s first leap (the outfitters also offer a weeklong package that includes tent camping on the polar plateau and, for most thrill-seekers, a heart-pounding $36,000 price tag). The minus-25-degree wind chill put a deep freeze on any regrets about aging.

“Only a handful of people on earth can say they’ve skydived over Antarctica,” says Kush. “My biggest fear isn’t dying. It’s being stuck to a recliner when I’m 85.”

Kush and his teammates’ adventure raised money for Folds of Honor, a charity that provides scholarships for families of fallen or disabled service members. “When you retire from the military, everything stops,” says Kush. “You’ve got to find a new mission. I realized I can still live a great story, and that’s what I’m trying to do.”

“Experiencing early signs of an aging body in middle age may awaken a desire to get started on one’s bucket list,” says psychologist Margie Lachman, a professor at Brandeis University.

Indeed, the marketing firm Grand View Research reports that travelers in their 50s now make up 26% of the adventure tourism market. And adventure companies are cashing in on the midlife itch, selling high-risk, high-budget escapes to travelers eager to prove they’ve still got what it takes—plus ibuprofen and a heating pad. An Australian outfit literally called Mid Life Adventures runs long-distance motorcycle treks through Mongolia, India and Nepal, pitching them as unforgettable experiences for 40- to 70-year-olds with the time, money and desire to step beyond their norm.

Destination tastings

Nowadays, foodies venture far beyond Napa Valley for wine tours, Kentucky for bourbon pilgrimages and Portugal for olive oil tastings.

“Picklesburgh is something you have to attend at least once in your life,” says Lori of Hanover, Maryland, who visited the multiday pickle festival in Pittsburgh with three friends. The event features everything from pickle ice cream to pickle beer, pickle-themed Christmas ornaments, a pickle-eating contest and pickle bobbing. Lori and her group sampled pickle margaritas, fried pickles and pickle fudge—a funky blend of tangy, salty and perplexing. Attendees can also ride a bucking mechanical pickle under a gigantic pickle-shaped balloon. One YouTube reviewer called Picklesburgh “an experience I will always relish.”

“What we eat and drink can bring us joy,” says Cheryl Stanley, a senior lecturer at Cornell University’s Peter and Stephanie Nolan School of Hotel Administration. “And traveling so we can expand our knowledge about a place, food or beverage, or to meet a cheese maker or sake producer, can take that joy to the next level by creating lasting memories.”

In Waikiki, Hawaii, the Spam Jam Festival celebrates the state’s favorite canned meat with Spam tacos, Spam sushi and Spam wontons, drawing tens of thousands of devoted fans. “If you’re looking for creativity, this is it,” commented Tiffany from Fairfax, Virginia, on Yelp. “I’m a food explorer, so I wanted to try all the unique Spam offerings—and I’ll admit, I didn’t really like most of them,” she confesses, although she concedes that the Spam Wellington “was pretty good.”

Some tastings veer toward outright daring. In Wiscasset, Maine, the annual Sustainable Supper with Edible Insects at the Maine Tasting Center begins with a cocktail hour featuring drinks like an Oaxacan margarita rimmed with crushed toasted agave worms. Rather than formal hors d’oeuvres, guests nibble on toasted grasshoppers and roasted crickets. A four-course dinner features dishes like mealworm dumplings with beetle chili crisp, and the event ends with party favors of insect-based sweets, including scorpion suckers.

One guest, Sam Chatto of South Portland, Maine, says the ants speckling the cornbread “looked like poppy seeds.” Then came the real test. “A taco with beetles and mealworms was visually intimidating,” he says. “There was no mistake that you were eating bugs.” So, how were they? “Great,” says Chatto. “They may have added a crunch, but in reality, that faded into the background.”

When all is said and done, it’s not just a matter of taste. It’s a matter of place—and how far you’re willing to go.

articles.title: What’s Your Greatest Wish for America?
articles.content:
在美国250岁生日之际，想象一个巨大的蛋糕，上面插着数百万支蜡烛，每支蜡烛代表一个愿望。现在想象其中一支蜡烛是你的。

你对美国的愿望是什么？纽约历史学会提出了这个重要问题，来自全美50个州和华盛顿特区的超过一万人分享了他们对国家未来的美好想法。

请继续阅读一些最鼓舞人心的留言，访问OnOur250th.org分享你自己的愿望。

服务

我希望我们能恢复曾经被称为公共服务的东西的信任——即民选代表为全体人民服务。不是为自己服务，不是为了个人经济利益或权力。而是为了人民的利益。——华盛顿特区 珍妮弗

希望

我们积极追求伟大的、雄心勃勃的未来，值得我们的孩子、他们的孩子以及后代去追寻。——马里兰州 卡拉

我的希望是一个美国，每个人都因其才能、天赋和智慧而受到尊重。一个我们自豪地支持美国宪法作为民主最佳范例的美国。一个多元化、公平和包容的国家。——弗吉尼亚州 芭芭拉

我们目前面临的问题只有在共同努力下才能解决。美国人民总是团结起来解决冲突。让我们现在就这样做吧。——佛罗里达州 谢丽

平等

希望女性能够获得同工同酬。——俄亥俄州 伊薇

我希望世界和平、人人善良、心灵宁静。——德克萨斯州 艾伦

社区

我希望我们能重新重视学习和知识，支持我们的图书馆和博物馆。我希望我们能重新重视社区建设，包容他人，即使他们与你不同。——阿肯色州 摩根

在美国250周年纪念之际，我希望美国人民能更多地与邻居和同胞进行社交互动，同时大幅减少社交媒体。活在真实世界里。不再是屏幕。——伊利诺伊州 韦斯利

民主

一个繁荣的民主制度、健康的公共辩论、强大而独立的媒体，以及对自由、正义和平等这些永恒理想的坚持追求。愿我们继续共同努力，让梦想成真，尊重我们共同的人性。——密歇根州 克里斯汀

我祈祷我们能回归先辈们的愿望和他们创建“上帝之下统一、不可分割、自由和正义属于所有人”的国家的初衷。——阿肯色州 邦妮

我希望民主在我的国家闪耀光芒。我计划成为解决方案的一部分！——康涅狄格州 加里

我对美国的愿望是希望每个人都尊重和欣赏我们的差异，这些差异使我们团结而不是分裂。我们在一起会更强大！让我们为了所有人的共同利益而共同努力！——宾夕法尼亚州 汤姆

团结

我喜欢一个大型社区聚餐的想法——代表所有美食传统和背景，展现美国这个大熔炉！——犹他州 梅莉

我希望我们能变得更加包容每个人的差异，让每个人都能安全地被接纳。——新罕布什尔州 克里斯

前行

过去只是为未来提供信息，只要我们能勇敢地向前看，承认我们多元化和差异所带来的力量，未来必将伟大。——缅因州 梅兰妮

美国250岁生日快乐，我热爱的土地。最美好的还在后头！！——新泽西州 米歇尔

激励

我的愿望是美国永远像山巅上闪耀的光芒。诚实加机会加努力等于成功。当我们尝试的时候，我们的国家仍然为我们提供所需的一切。——爱达荷州 蒂娜

谢谢你，美国，感谢所有光荣的歌曲、风景和声音。继续奏响你的乐章——教导孩子们，保持坚强。——纽约州 洛基

感恩

美国，感谢你赐予我们在称之为家园的美丽土地上生活和爱的自由！——南卡罗来纳州 亚历克西斯

爱国

希望美国人学会热爱这个美丽的国家，即使你不喜欢当权的那个人。对于所有人，无论民主党还是共和党，都能团结成一个整体，首先我们是美国人，其次才是其他。——加利福尼亚州 瓦尔

美国250岁生日快乐！我希望年轻人的爱国情怀能够增强。我希望贫困和无家可归的现象能够结束。——田纳西州 斯蒂芬妮

信任我们的理由

在《读者文摘》，我们致力于生产高质量内容，由具有专业知识和经验的作家撰写，并咨询相关合格的专家。我们依靠可靠的一手来源，包括政府和专业组织、学术机构，以及作家在适当情况下的个人经历。我们验证所有事实和数据，用可信的来源支持，并随着时间推移不断回顾，确保它们保持准确和最新。了解更多关于我们团队、贡献者和编辑政策的信息。
articles.summary:
On our country’s 250th birthday, imagine a big cake with millions of candles, each representing a wish. Now imagine that one of those candles is yours.

What’s your wish for America? The New York Historical asked that all-important question, and more than 10,000 people, in all 50 states and Washington, D.C., shared their best thoughts for our nation’s future.

Read on for some of the most inspiring messages shared, and visit OnOur250th.org to post your own.

Get Reader’s Digest’s Read Up newsletter for more inspiring stories, fun facts, humor, cleaning, travel and tech all week long.

Service

  • I hope that we will restore thefaith in what used to be known as public service; that is, elected representatives serving all the people. Not self-service, not for personal financial gain or power. For the benefit of the people. —Jennifer from Washington, D.C.

Hope

  • That we are actively pursuing big, bold aspirational futures that are worthy of our children and their children and the generations to come. —Kara from Maryland
  • My hope is for an America where everyone is respected for their talents, gifts and intelligence. An America where we proudly support the Constitution of the United States as the best example of democracy. A diverse, equitable and inclusive country. —Barbara from Virginia
  • The problems we currently face can onlybe resolved if we work together. The people of America have always joined together to resolve conflict. Let’s do it now. —Sherryfrom Florida

Equality

  • For women to be paid equally. —Evie from Ohio
  • I wish for world peace, kindness and serenity for all. —Ellen from Texas

Community

  • I wish that we could return to celebrating learning and knowledge by supporting our libraries and museums. I wish that we could return to making communities and the acceptance of others, even if they are different. —Morgan from Arkansas
  • On this, America’s quarter millennium anniversary, I wish the American people more social interaction with neighbors and countrymen, while cutting down dramatically on social media. Live in the real world. No more screens. —Wesley from Illinois

Democracy

  • A thriving democracy, healthy public debate, strong and independent press, and the relentless pursuit of the enduring ideals of liberty, justice and equality for all. May we keep working together to make the dream real and honor our shared humanity. —Kristin from Michigan
  • I pray that we return to the desires of our forefathers and their intention to create “one nation, under God, indivisible, with liberty and justice for all.” —Bonnie from Arkansas
  • I wish for democracy to shine bright in my country. I plan to be part of the solution! —Gary from Connecticut
  • My wish for America is for everyone to respect and appreciate our differencesto unite us and not divide us. We are stronger together! Let us work together for the common good of all! —Tom from Pennsylvania

Togetherness

  • I love the idea of a huge neighborhood potluck—representing all culinary traditions and backgrounds that represent the great American melting pot! —Meli from Utah
  • I hope that we can become more accepting of everyone’s differences and make room for everyone to be safe and accepted. —Chris from New Hampshire

Onward

  • The past merely informs our future, which is destined to be great if we can look courageously forward, acknowledging the power that comes from our diversity and differences. —Melanie from Maine
  • Happy 250th birthday, America, land that I love. The best is yet to come!! —Michelle from New Jersey

Inspiration

  • My wish is for America the Beautiful to forever stand as that shining light on a hill. Honesty plus opportunity plus hard work equals success. Our nation still provides us with everything we need when we try. —Tina  from Idaho
  • Thank you, America, for all the glorious songs and sights and sounds. Keep making your music—teach the children, stay strong. —Rocky from New York

Gratitude

  • America, thank you for the freedom to live and love in the beautiful land we call home! —Alexis from South Carolina

Patriotism

  • For Americans to learn to love this beautiful nation even if you don’t like the guy or gal in charge. For all, whether Democrats or Republicans, to come together as one and be Americans first before we are anything else. —Val from California
  • Happy 250th birthday, America! I wish for patriotism to increase among young people.I wish for an end to poverty and homelessness. —Stephanie from Tennessee

Why trust us

We rely on reputable primary sources, including government and professional organizations and academic institutions as well as our writers’ personal experiences where appropriate. We verify all facts and data, back them with credible sourcing and revisit them over time to ensure they remain accurate and up to date.
